metadata.title: Base test review rubric
metadata.description: Default severity rules for /gaia-test-review covering test structure, mock usage, fixture management, test data, and integration test isolation. Layer 1 of the four-layer rubric composition.

severity_rules.pattern: Test body lacks a clear Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) or Given-When-Then sectioning — setup, action, and verification are interleaved.
severity_rules.description: Interleaved test bodies obscure the intent and make failures harder to diagnose.
severity_rules.remediation: Group setup, action, and assertions into clearly separated stanzas (blank-line or comment-delimited).

severity_rules.pattern: Test file mixes unit and integration scope without separation — fast in-memory tests share a file with tests that hit a real database or network.
severity_rules.description: Mixed-scope files force the slowest test to gate every developer's edit-compile loop.
severity_rules.remediation: Split the file into `*.unit.spec` and `*.integration.spec` (or equivalent) so suites can run independently.

severity_rules.pattern: Single test exceeds 100 executable lines or covers more than one behaviour — long, multi-purpose test cases.
severity_rules.description: Long test cases bury the single behaviour they should isolate and slow root-cause analysis on failure.
severity_rules.remediation: Decompose into focused cases — one behaviour per test, shared setup in `beforeEach`.

severity_rules.pattern: Test mocks the system under test itself (mocking the very class or function being verified).
severity_rules.description: Mocking the SUT verifies the mock, not the real behaviour — the test is vacuous.
severity_rules.remediation: Mock collaborators only; let the SUT execute its real logic against the mocked boundaries.

severity_rules.pattern: Mock is configured to return a value never asserted against — the mock setup is unused or only verifies that the mock was called.
severity_rules.description: Unused mock setup is dead code and obscures which interactions actually matter.
severity_rules.remediation: Remove the unused setup or convert it into an assertion on the collaborator interaction.

severity_rules.pattern: Test asserts on internal mock interactions (`expect(mock).toHaveBeenCalledWith(...)`) instead of the SUT's observable outcomes.
severity_rules.description: Over-specified mock interactions couple the test to the implementation and break under benign refactors.
severity_rules.remediation: Prefer state-based assertions on the SUT's outputs and side effects; use interaction assertions sparingly.

severity_rules.pattern: Test creates a large fixture inline at the top of the file, duplicating the same shape across multiple tests.
severity_rules.description: Inline duplicated fixtures drift over time and increase the cost of changing the underlying type.
severity_rules.remediation: Extract a builder or factory function that returns the fixture; allow each test to override the relevant fields.

severity_rules.pattern: Fixture data references a non-deterministic source (current time, random seed, environment variable) without freezing or seeding.
severity_rules.description: Non-deterministic fixtures make assertions intermittent and obscure root cause on failure.
severity_rules.remediation: Freeze the clock or seed the RNG inside the fixture builder; document the seed.

severity_rules.pattern: Test relies on production-like data (real customer record, real PII, real API key) instead of synthesised inputs.
severity_rules.description: Production-like data in tests risks PII leakage, complicates onboarding, and couples test results to production state.
severity_rules.remediation: Use a test-data factory that generates synthesised values — never copy production rows into the suite.

severity_rules.pattern: Test data is hard-coded as a magic literal whose meaning is opaque (`expect(result).toEqual(42)` with no rationale).
severity_rules.description: Magic-literal expected values do not explain why that value is correct, making future maintenance error-prone.
severity_rules.remediation: Compute the expected value from named constants or document the rationale alongside the assertion.

severity_rules.pattern: Integration test shares a database, message broker, or cache with the rest of the suite without per-test reset or per-suite container.
severity_rules.description: Shared external state across integration tests creates cross-test pollution and flaky-by-order failures.
severity_rules.remediation: Provision a per-suite container (Testcontainers) or wrap each test in a transaction that rolls back on teardown.

severity_rules.pattern: Integration test depends on a hard-coded port, hostname, or environment variable instead of a discovery mechanism.
severity_rules.description: Hard-coded coordinates break in CI when the resource lives at a different address and complicate parallel execution.
severity_rules.remediation: Read the address from the test container or a discovery API; never hard-code in the test body.
